coredata.dc:title: Novel microwave absorbers based on polypyrrole and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rahal, Michel
Atassi, Yomen
Ali, Nassim Nasser
Alghoraibi, Ibrahim
coredata.dc:description: Herein, polypyrrole/carbon quantum dots (PPyCQDs) composite microwave absorbers (MA) were prepared via in-situ polymerization of pyrrole in presence of CQDs. The structural, morphological and electrical characterization of the samples were performed using X-Ray diffractometry, infra-red spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, AFM, SEM and DC conductivity measurements. Microwave absorbing properties were investigated by measuring the reflection loss (RL) within the range of 2–8 GHz as a function of thickness and loading percentage. Results revealed an increase in DC conductivity and RL in parallel with CQDs content increase. The minimum reflection loss was −27.82 dB at 6.93 GHz for a matching thickness of 3.25 mm. The bandwidth under −10dB was 2.34 GHz for a loading percentage of 25% in a host matrix of paraffin. The use of CQDs in the preparation of MA has not been reported before, and the current microwave absorber holds great promise for the design of new generations of microwave absorbers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave absorbance
Polypyrrole
Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of CQD addition to PPy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of: CQD, PPy, PPyCQD25, PPyCQD50, PPyCQD100, PPyCQD150, PPyCQD200.
IR Spectra of: CQD, PPy0, PPyCQD25, PPyCQD50, PPyCQD100, PPyCQD150, PPyCQD200.
2D, 3D images and cross-section profiles of a) PPy, b) CQD and c) PPyCQD.
SEM Images of a) PPy, b) PPyCQD at different magnification scales.
UV–Vis Spectra for: CQD, PPy, PPyCQD25, PPyCQD50, PPyCQD100, PPyCQD150, PPyCQD200.
RL curves of CQD and PPy with loading percentage 20% at different thicknesses: a) CQD 6.0 mm, b) PPy 6.0 mm, c) PPy 5.0 mm, d) PPy 4.5 mm, e) PPy 4.0 mm.
RL curves of PPyCQD25 with loading percentage 20% at different thicknesses: a) 6.0 mm, b) 5.5 mm, c) 5.0 mm, d) 4.75 mm.
RL curves of PPyCQD50 with loading percentage 20% at different thicknesses: a) 5.75 mm, b) 5.0 mm, c) 4.5 mm, d) 4.0 mm, e) 3.75 mm, f) 3.5 mm.
RL curves of PPyCQD100 with loading percentage 20% at different thicknesses: a) 6.0 mm, b) 5.5 mm, c) 5.0 mm, d) 4.5 mm, e) 4.0 mm, f) 3.0 mm.
RL curves of PPyCQD150 with loading percentage 20% at different thicknesses: a) 6.0 mm, b) 5.5 mm, c) 5.0 mm, d) 4.75 mm, e) 4.5 mm, f) 4.25 mm, g) 4.0 mm, h) 3.75 mm, i) 3.5 mm, j) 3.25 mm.
RL curves of PPyCQD200 with loading percentage 20% a) 6.0 mm, b) 5.5 mm, c) 5.0 mm, d) 4.75 mm, e) 4.5 mm, f) 4.25 mm, g) 4.0 mm, h) 3.75 mm, i) 3.5 mm.
Correlation between min RL and CQDs content at different thicknesses for 20% loading percentage.
RL curves of PPyCQD200 with loading percentage 25% a) 6.0 mm, b) 5.5 mm, c) 4.75 mm, d) 4.0 mm, e) 3.5 mm, f) 3.25 mm, g) 3.0 mm.
RL curves of PPyCQD200 with loading percentage 30% a) 5.5 mm, b) 5.0 mm, c) 4.75 mm, d) 4.5 mm, e) 4.0 mm, f) 3.5 mm, g) 3.0 mm.
Min RL and bandwidth at different loading percentages for PPyCQD200 composite.
Sample codes and amounts of CQDs.
Conductivity measurements of samples.
Microwave absorption behaviour of pristine PPy and CQDs.
Microwave absorption performance of PPyCQD25, 50, 100, 150, 200, with loading percentage 20%.
Microwave absorption performance of PPyCQD200 at loading percentages 25% and 30%.
Microwave absorbing properties comparison with literature.

head.ce:title: Novel microwave absorbers based on polypyrrole and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology, Damascus, Syria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology, Damascus, Syria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Damascus University, Damascus, Syria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Damascus University, Damascus, Syria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, polypyrrole/carbon quantum dots (PPyCQDs) composite microwave absorbers (MA) were prepared via in-situ polymerization of pyrrole in presence of CQDs. The structural, morphological and electrical characterization of the samples were performed using X-Ray diffractometry, infra-red spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, AFM, SEM and DC conductivity measurements. Microwave absorbing properties were investigated by measuring the reflection loss (RL) within the range of 2–8 GHz as a function of thickness and loading percentage. Results revealed an increase in DC conductivity and RL in parallel with CQDs content increase. The minimum reflection loss was −27.82 dB at 6.93 GHz for a matching thickness of 3.25 mm. The bandwidth under −10dB was 2.34 GHz for a loading percentage of 25% in a host matrix of paraffin. The use of CQDs in the preparation of MA has not been reported before, and the current microwave absorber holds great promise for the design of new generations of microwave absorbers.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, combination of multifunctional materials have attracted a great deal of attention due to their potential applications in many fields, where the combination of two multifunctional materials can enhance a property of one component or can produce new functions [1,2]. Polypyrrole (PPy), a conductive conjugated polymer, is preferable to be used due to its ease of synthesis, tunable electrical properties, environmental stability, which allowed it to be extensively used in wide range of applications as a neuronal conductor [3], ions removal from drinking water [4], and in electronic devices [5]. It was also studied as an electromagnetic absorbing material due to its dielectric properties, in conjunction with magnetic materials as ferrites [6]. Due to the difference of phases properties, sometimes combining conductive polymers with conventional microwave shielding materials as metals, has many technological drawbacks as their high specific gravity and difficulty in formulation [7], aggregation phenomenon due to high surface activity, and probability of chemical corrosion [8]. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), a newly fluorescent material, were studied in many applications based on its fluorescence property in metal ions sensing [9], vitamins detection [10], and cellular imaging [11]. PPy is used alongside with CQDs in non-fluorescent applications as for the detection of picric acid [12], volatile organics detection in food [13], and supercapacitors [14,15], where the organic structural similarity can bypass technological problems as the homogeneity. In the current work, we investigate the effect of combining CQDs with PPy to enhance its electromagnetic absorbing properties. Traditionally, magnetic and dielectric lossy materials are used to prepare microwave absorbers. Spinel and hexagonal ferrites are widely used as magnetic lossy materials, whereas inherently conductive polymers including polypyrrole, polyaniline, polythiophene to name a few, are commonly used as dielectric lossy materials [6,16]. A literature survey has revealed that only scanty literature is available on the use of semiconductor quantum dots, such as Cd–Se quantum dots, in microwave absorption applications [17,18]. CQDs known for their environmental friendliness, low cost, excellent electrical properties, and simple synthetic routes have not been used before in the preparation microwave absorbers. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of the effect of carbon quantum dots on microwave absorption performance of polypyrrole.
-Pyrrole monomer solution was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Ammonium Peroxydisulfate, Chloroform, Citric Acid and Thiourea were obtained from Merck, Water is deionized and doubly distilled.

XRD patterns were registered using X-ray diffractometer (Philips, X'pert), using CuKα (λ = 0.154 nm), in the range of 10–45° by a step of 0.02°/s. UV–Vis spectra were performed using JASCO, V-350 UV–Vis spectrophotometer in the range of 280–1200 nm. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using Bruker, Vector 22 spectrophotometer in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. Morphology was determined by Tescan Vega-II XMU5136 variable pressure Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), equipped with X-Ray detector for elemental analysis. Surface Topology was determined by Nanosurf easyScan2 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM). Scans were performed in contact mode. DC electrical conductivity measurement was conducted using four-probe Van Der Pauw technique via KEITHELY-220 programmable current source and KEYTHELY-617 programmable electrometer. The powder was compressed at 700 MPa using hydraulic press, the conductivity was calculated using Eq. (1) [19]:
(1)
σ =
1
2 π s
I V
Where s is the distance between two adjacent probes (s = 0.156 cm), I is the current intensity (mA), v is the voltage (mV). The reflection loss (RL) of the samples was evaluated using vector network analyser (VNA, Rhode and Schwarz, R&S ZVH) in the C band by the waveguide method.

CQDs were synthesized in one-step microwave method. Briefly, thiourea (1.2 g) and citric acid (1 g) were dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water in a 100 mL beaker, stirred until total dissolution for 1 h. Then the beaker transferred into domestic microwave, and 400 W power was applied for 10 min. A black-brownish powder was formed, cooled down and dissolved in 15 mL of distilled water, homogenized by ultrasonication for 15 min in order to release carbon quantum dots from black powder, and centrifuged on 6000 rpm up to 60 min to separate dissolved carbon quantum dots from large agglomerates, filtered using 0.2 μm filter papers, placed into Vivaspin centrifuge columns and re-centrifuged (to separate remaining agglomerates), then heated in an oven at 45 °C under reduced pressure (0.01 MPa) for 24 h, then a solid brownish CQD was obtained. Reaction yield was 30%.

Various amounts of CQDs were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water (DW) in a 100 mL beaker, then 1 g of pyrrole monomers were dissolved and stirred for 1 h. The beaker placed into an ice bath and the reaction medium temperature was adjusted between 0-5 °C. Appropriate quantity of APS was dissolved in 40 mL of DW in a beaker, placed into the ice bath, then APS solution was dropped for 10 min into pyrrole solution, with continuous stirring for 1 h, and the reaction extended for 24 h. Thereafter, black dispersed particles were formed, filtered using vacuum pump, washed with water and ethanol for three times, to remove unreacted monomers, then placed into oven at 45 °C under reduced pressure (0.01 MPa) for 24 h. A fine black powder was stored in the dark until further investigation.

In order to prepare a monolayer microwave absorber, the nanocomposite samples were mixed homogenously with a molten paraffin wax. Subsequently, the single layer microwave samples were molded in cylindrical coaxial mold, inner diameter 3.0 mm, outer diameter 7.0 mm, sample length between 6 and 7 mm. Samples were molded at different loading percentages (w/w%) of sample to wax and at different thicknesses. In this work, five types of composite samples were investigated, in addition to pristine PPy and CQD samples. The sample codes of the composites are listed in Table 1 .

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns for PPy, CQDs and composites. CQDs and PPy patterns showed a broad hump centred at 16.6° and 25.4° respectively, which indicates the amorphous structure of CQD and PPy. The halo of PPy can be ascribed to the scattering at the interplanar spacing due to short-range order [20–23].

For XRD patterns of composites, CQDs' halo wasn’t clear, maybe due to low intensity caused by CQDs low concentration relatively to PPy. Moreover, PPyCQDs' halos were broadened as CQDs content increases and they centred around 24.4°. This could be attributed to the fact that the molecular structure of PPy was disrupted, due to the mutual interactions between CQDs and PPy chains.

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of prepared samples. CQD absorption bands are: symmetric and non-symmetric stretching vibration for N–H bond at 3188 cm−1, stretching vibration for C–H bond at 2887 cm−1, symmetric stretching vibration for CO bond at 1704 cm−1 which is concerned for carbonyl group conjugated with nitrogen by amide group, bending vibration for N–H group in amides at 1669 cm−1, bands between 1000 and 1400 cm−1 concerned with bending vibration for C–S, C–O, C–H, and C–N bonds, bending vibration for S–OR at 950 cm−1, bending vibration for C–H in R2CCH2 group at 831 cm−1, and bending vibration for C–N bond at 622 cm−1 [10,11,24]. For PPy spectrum: a small absorption band of CO at 1710 cm−1 is related to low oxidation degree on pyrrole ring, two absorption bands at for pyrrole ring vibration at 1550 and 1450 cm−1, stretching band for CC at 1316 cm−1, stretching band for conjugated C–N at 1150 cm−1, in-plane vibration band of = C–N at 1040 cm−1, vibration band of C–H at 911 cm−1, two absorption bands at 615 and 782 for S–O vibrations, may be due to oxidant residues [12,25–27].

For PPyCQD spectrum, CQDs characteristic bands aren't clear enough, may be due to low CQD loading percentage relatively to PPy, while a gradual decrease observed in pyrrole ring vibration at 1550 cm−1 band of PPy, this may be due to creation of physical interaction between PPy and CQD, which would decrease vibration amplitude of bonds in pyrrole ring. As it is clearly shown in PPy200 spectrum that there is a large decrease in band intensities, which supports the hypothesis of physical interaction between PPY and CQDs.

AFM imaging was employed in order to investigate the topology of materials. Figure 3 shows 2D, 3D and cross-section profiles of a-pristine PPy, b-CQD and c-PPyCQD. It can be seen that pristine PPy has granular appearance with large agglomerates with radius between 600 and 750 nm, while CQDs appear as spherical shaped particles ranging between 5 and 25 nm, and PPyCQDs composite has porous granular structure with interconnections between granules, with sizes between 200 and 300 nm.

Surface roughness (
R a
), as an another indicator for particle size, was calculated by EasyScan 2 software, for 5 × 5 μm size surfaces for PPy and PPyCQD, and for 2 × 2 μm size surface for CQD.
R a
of PPy is 118 nm, while
R a
of CQD is 2.1 nm, but
R a
of PPyCQD has dramatically reduced to 20.08 nm. This indicates that PPyCQD has smoother surface than pristine PPy, due to the presence of CQDs.

Fig. 4 shows SEM micrographs of a) PPy and b) PPyCQD. It is shown that pristine PPy morphology consists of compact aggregates of spherical shaped particles, with ~600 nm in diameter. This morphology is in agreement with literature [22,28,29]. The morphology of PPyCQD200 composite is also aggregated granules structure, but less compact in comparison with pristine PPy, with branched strands around granules edges, which reflects higher porosity, thereby higher surface area to volume ratio. This can be explained that the presence of CQD affected the polymerization of pyrrole monomers, where CQDs play role of template for nucleation and growth, where PPy chains grew on these nucleation sites. EDX spectra represent characteristic dispersed energy for each element within each material. The analysis reveals that the carbon and sulphur weight percentages have increased in PPyCQD, in comparison with pristine PPy, while oxygen and nitrogen weight percentage have decreased, which demonstrates the effect of sulphur-doped CQDs presence within PPy. EDX spectra are shown in Supplementary Information.

The electrical conductivity of PPy, and all samples at room temperature are listed in Table 2 . Pristine PPy has relatively poor conductivity. By adding increased amounts of CQD to PPy, the conductivity raised, this can be explained that the CQD playing role of dopant. Pristine PPy has intrinsic conductivity which comes from few free electrons, while doping with CQD can create charges carriers that enhances conductivity of the PPy [30].

Figure 5 shows the UV–vis spectra of prepared samples. From Fig. 5, CQDs have an absorption band at 340 nm, corresponding to n → π* electronic transition of CO groups [31]. As for PPy spectrum, there's an absorption band at 343 nm, which is for π → π* electronic transition between CC bonds. In addition, there was a large absorption tail between 600 and 1200 nm, called free carrier tail, it appears when π electrons are conjugated along the polymeric chain which means. The presence this free carrier tail is indicator that the chains are extended, not random coiled and the conjugated polymers are well doped [5,32].

The reflection loss (RL) of an electromagnetic waves from a surface of a monolayer microwave absorber backed by an ideal conductor is given by Eq. (2) [33]:
(2)
R L
( d B )
= 20 log
|
Z
i n
−
Z 0
Z
i n
+
Z 0
|
Where
Z 0
≈ 378
Ω
represents the impedance of air,
Z
i n
is the input impedance of the absorber, and it is related to the electromagnetic parameters (complex relative permeability and permittivity) of the absorbing medium by Eq. (3) [34,35]:
(3)
Z
i n
=
Z 0
μ ε
tanh
( j
2 π f d
c
ε μ
)
Where,
μ
is the complex permeability,
ε
is the complex permittivity, c is the speed of light,
j
is the
− 1
,
f
is the frequency of the electromagnetic wave,
d
is the absorber thickness.

When RL is less than −10 dB, the material absorbs 90% of the incoming wave, and when RL equals to −20, the materials absorb 99% of the incoming wave, and so on. The threshold −10 dB was selected in all samples. Another parameter is important is the bandwidth below –10 dB; the wider is the better.

In a preliminary experiment, not shown here, there was no significant microwave absorption performance for PPy alone when the loading percentage was 10%. Figure 6 and Table 3 show that by increasing loading percentage of PPy to 20%, at different thicknesses, RL slightly exceeds −10 dB threshold. However, CQDs alone didn't show significant absorbing properties at this loading percentage, Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQD addition to PPy
ce:section.ce:para: When adding small amount of CQDs to PPy (PPyCQD25), there was no noticeable change in the absorption, but it is shifted to higher frequencies, as shown in Fig. 7 .

With the gradual addition of CQDs to PPy (PPyCQD50), there was a slight increase in absolute value of RL, and covered wide range of frequencies, from 4.0 to 6.7 GHz. The highest absorption (RL of −15.10 dB) and the largest bandwidth was (1.48 GHz) for a thickness of 3.50 mm, Fig. 8 .

Fig. 9 shows that the continuous addition of CQDs to PPy (PPYCQD100) results in slight enhancement in RL and bandwidth. The highest absorption (−16.2 dB) is achieved for a matching thickness of 5.00 mm and the largest bandwidth was 1.50 GHz at 4.00 mm, Table 4 .

Microwave absorption performance of the samples improves with continuous addition of CQDs to PPy, (PPyCQD150). RL enhanced and exceeded the −10 dB threshold, and covered wide range of frequencies, from 4.2 to 7.6 GHz. The highest absorption (−20.16 dB) was for 6.00 mm thickness and the widest bandwidth (2.09 GHz) for 3.25 mm thickness, this is shown in Fig. 10 .

When adding more CQDs to PPy (PPYCQD200), RL enhanced significantly, and covered wider range of frequencies, from 3.8 to 7.6 GHz. The Highest absorption was −32.80 dB for a sample thickness of 5.5 mm and the largest bandwidth was for 1.86 dB at 4.00 mm sample thickness, Fig. 11 .

Table 4 recapitulates the effects of CQDs content in PPyCQD composites on microwave absorption characteristics, namely the minimum reflection loss, the matching frequency and the bandwidth under −10 dB. The matching impedance depends on CQD/PPy ratio, loading percentage and the thickness of the sample. Therefore, the minimum reflection loss, the matching frequency and the bandwidth under −10dB change accordingly.

One can easily notice that as the content of CQDs increases (25, 50, 100, 150, 200 mg), the microwave absorption performance of the best sample, chosen from samples at the same CQDs content but at different thicknesses, enhances gradually (−11.10, −11.33, −15.10, −16.20, −20.16, −32.80 dB).

The significant enhancement in RL of −32.80 dB for PPyCQD200 sample in comparison to −11.80 dB for PPy sample alone, proves the beneficial role in combining PPy and CQDs. This improvement in microwave performance could be linked to interfacial polarization between PPy chains and CQDs and the electrical conductivity of both PPy and CQDs.

In fact, it is known that the dispersion behaviour of imaginary part of permittivity in materials is given according to Eq. (4) [16]:
(4)
ε ″
=
ε
r e l a x
″
+
σ
d c
2 π f
Where
ε
r e l a x
″
is the component of the imaginary permittivity linked to the contribution of polarization relaxations,
σ
d c
is the dc conductivity of the material and
f
is the frequency.

Hence, dielectric loss can be boosted in following several strategies: (i) raising the orientation polarization by controlling the microstructure of the dielectric component of the absorber, (ii) increasing the space charge or interface polarization by adjusting the morphology of microwave absorber, (iii) employing conductive materials with low percolation threshold to form micro currents through and electrical percolation network. This is reflected experimentally in the current work by: (1) The choice of PPy as an inherently conductive polymer with interesting electrical conductivity and important orientation polarization due to π electrons delocalization along the polymeric chain, (2) The choice of CQDs known for their interesting electrical conductivity, (3) The core-shell structures of the absorber by adopting the polymerization of pyrrole on CQDs in solution. The core-shell morphology promotes the role of interfacial polarization and therefore enhances the microwave absorption characteristics of the absorber.
Fig. 12 is a plot of Min RL vs sample thickness at different CQD/PPy ratios for a loading percentage of 20% as shown below:
One can easily conclude that the addition of CQDs enhances the reflection loss of PPy. The best impedance matching occurs for PPyCQD200.

Since the PPyCQD200 composite exhibits the best microwave absorption characteristics (RL = −32.80 dB, BW = 1.27 dB), the effect of increasing loading percentage was studied on it. Fig. 13 depicts the RL of PPyCQD200 at 25% loading percentage at different thicknesses. Tables 4 and 5 reveal that the samples of PPyCQD200 at 25% loading percentage, have no significant improvement in the RL in comparison with their performance at 20% loading percentage. However, the bandwidth under −10dB reaches 2.34 GHz with RL = −27.82 dB at 3.25 mm thickness. Fig. 14 depicts the RL of the PPyCQD200 at 30% loading percentage. It shows slightly improved bandwidth of 2.63 GHz at 3.00 mm thickness, but the RL reaches only −19.97 dB, Table 5.

This means that the best impedance matching occurs for PPyCQD200 sample at 25% loading percentage. PPyCQD200 sample exhibits the best microwave absorption performance with RL of −27.82 dB at 3.25 mm thickness and a bandwidth of 2.34 GHz around the matching frequency of 6.9 GHz. Further increase in loading results in heavier microwave absorber with less absorption performance.

When optimizing the design of novel microwave absorbers, one doesn't only optimize the minimum RL, but also the bandwidth and the matching frequency, by changing the loading percentage and the thickness. According to the final application of the absorber (shielding at a specified frequency, shielding with a wide bandwidth or requiring a lightweight absorber), one chooses the appropriate combination of loading percentage and thickness.

For the current absorber, the best impedance matching occurs for PPyCQD200 sample at 25% loading percentage. PPyCQD200 sample exhibits the best microwave absorption performance with RL of −27.82 dB at 3.25 mm thickness and a bandwidth of 2.34 GHz around the matching frequency of 6.9 GHz. Further increase in loading results in heavier microwave absorber with less absorption performance. Herein in Fig. 15 , the optimized microwave absorber at 25% loading percentage is a trade-off of min RL and large bandwidth.

For all the studied samples, the matching frequency shifts to higher frequencies as the thickness decreases. This tendency follows Eq. (5) which relates the matching frequency and the absorber thickness [36]:
(5)
t m
=
n c
4 f
|
μ r
|
|
ε r
|
( n = 1,2,3,4 )
where
t m
is the absorber thickness,
c
is the speed of light,
f
is the frequency,
|
μ r
|
is the permeability,
|
ε r
|
is the permittivity.

The reflection loss property of an MW absorber is determined by the complex permittivity (related to electrical properties) and complex permeability (related to magnetic properties). Since carbon materials have no significant magnetic properties, thereby the contribution of PPy and CQDs in magnetic loss is negligible [37]. This would lead to the idea that the MW absorption property of PPyCQD composites is mainly due to the electric polarization, which is related to the permittivity, with no or negligible contribution from magnetic permeability.

When a conjugated polymer is doped, it will become electrically conductive, this can be interpreted that CQDs can induce the creation of charge carriers; polarons and bipolarons along PPy chain, which was previously confirmed by the UV–Vis spectra. It was also observed from conductivity measurements that increasing amounts of CQDs has increased conductivity; number of charge carriers is increased; thus the ability to absorb the electromagnetic waves has enhanced compared to pristine PPy, this phenomenon was also observed when Polyaniline was doped with MWCNTs [38]. This is described by Eqs. (4) and (6) [35,39]:
(6)
ε ∗
=
ε '
− j
ε
' '
Where
ε ∗
is the complex permittivity,
ε '
is the real permittivity,
ε
' '
is the imaginary permittivity (Dielectric loss).

From equation (4), it is clear that the imaginary permittivity (identical to the energy loss in the form of thermal energy), is needed to as large as it can be [6], and it is related to the polarization and the conductivity of the absorbing material, and decreases when the frequency increases.

Dielectric properties of the material results from ionic, electronic, orientational and space charge polarization. In composite systems, due to heterogeneity of the composite, the contribution to the space charge polarization dominates. In doped conjugated polymers strong polarization and relaxation effects appear due to polaron/bipolaron system [36], that is mobile and free to move along the chain and orientational polarization due to bound charges [38].

In Polypyrrole case, strong polarization occurs due to the presence of polaron/bipolaron and another bounds charge. In present PPyCQD composite, the contribution to dielectric constant and dielectric loss also occurs due to interfacial polarization and its relaxation as the CQDs separated by long polymer chains giving rise to heterogeneity, hopping of electrons and the relaxation due to interfacial polarization all are responsible for the oscillatory behaviour of absorption in the composite [40]. Electronic polarization can be described in the form of electron tunnelling between composite components, where due to the polarization this would transfer electrons from one component to another [41].

In comparison with literature, choosing those investigating the microwave absorption performance in the range 2–18 GHz, there was either slight absorbing properties of PPy composites in the frequency range between 2 and 8 GHz, or the absorbing properties were not effective in this range and the matching frequency is located in the X band, Table 6 [8,42–46]. Their loading percentage is relatively higher than the current absorber. PPyCQD composite successfully absorbed MW in the range of 2–8 GHz. It exhibits good absorption and relatively large wideband at relatively low loading percentage. This suggest that the current absorber holds great promise for shielding applications in the 2–8 GHz.

PPyCQD composites were prepared and characterized by XRD, FT-IR spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, AFM, SEM-EDX and DC conductivity measurements. For the first time, PPyCQD composites were successfully used as microwave absorbers. The results proved that the addition of CQDs to PPy enhances the microwave absorption performance of PPy alone. In fact, the appropriate combination of two dielectric lossy materials results in better microwave absorption performance of the composites. The RLs were correlated to both CQDs content and loading percentage of PPyCQD in paraffin. The optimum microwave absorber was obtained for PPyCQD200 at relatively low thickness of 3.25 mm and a loading percentage of 25%. The minimum RL was −27.82 dB at 6.93 GHz, and the bandwidth under −10 dB was 2.34 GHz. The enhancement of microwave absorption properties of the composites is attributed to the synergistic effect of the two dielectric loss components of the absorbers, namely PPy and CQDs. This type of composites could be considered as a promising material for electromagnetic shielding applications in the microwave bands.

Michel Rahal: Methodology, Visualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yomen Atassi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Nassim Nasser Ali: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Ibrahim Alghoraibi: Data curation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declares that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2020.123491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional materials: engineering applications and processing challenges
Int. J. Adv. Manuf. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Salonitis K, Pandremenos J, Paralikas J, Chryssolouris G. Multifunctional materials: Engineering applications and processing challenges. Int J Adv Manuf Technol. 2010;49(5-8):803-826. doi:10.1007/s00170-009-2428-6
